steps.prompt: Which words are the participles functioning as predicates?

hints.nihil, Horum: 'nihil' is a pronoun (nothing) and 'Horum' is a demonstrative genitive (of these things)
hints.modo, quidem: 'modo' is an adverb (only) and 'quidem' is a particle (even/indeed)
hints.sed, ne: 'sed' is a conjunction (but) and 'ne' here is part of 'ne … quidem' (not even)

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'effectum' (as a verb) translates to?

hints.to affect: 'to affect' is not a standard Latin meaning — 'efficere' means 'to accomplish/bring about'

hints.facio, facere, feci, factum: These are the parts of 'facere' (to do/make) — the base verb without the ex- prefix
hints.afficio, afficere, affeci, affectum: These are the parts of 'afficere' (to affect/influence) — a different compound
hints.conficio, conficere, confeci, confectum: These are the parts of 'conficere' (to complete/finish) — a different compound

hints.Not at all: 'Not at all' is 'minime' or 'nihil omnino' — 'ne … quidem' specifically means 'not even'
hints.Nor indeed: 'Nor indeed' is 'neque vero' — 'ne … quidem' means 'not even' with emphasis on the word between them
hints.Not only: 'Not only' is 'non solum/modo' — 'ne … quidem' means 'not even'

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural would be 'hi/hae/haec' — 'Horum' with -orum ending is genitive
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'his' — 'Horum' is genitive plural
hints.Accusative: Accusative plural would be 'hos/has/haec' — 'Horum' is genitive

steps.prompt: What is the structure 'non modo … sed ne … quidem'?
steps.target: Not only not X, but not even Y (a fortiori argument)
choices: Not only not X, but not even Y (a fortiori argument)

hints.Both X and Y: 'Both X and Y' is 'et … et …' — this structure escalates denial
hints.Either X or Y: 'Either X or Y' is 'aut … aut …' — this is a denial structure
hints.Neither X nor Y: 'Neither … nor' is 'neque … neque …' — 'non modo … sed ne … quidem' specifically escalates: not only not done, but not even thought of

hints.cogo, cogere, coegi, coactum: These are the parts of 'cogere' (to compel/gather)
hints.cognosco, cognoscere, cognovi, cognitum: These are the parts of 'cognoscere' (to learn/know)
hints.colligo, colligere, collegi, collectum: These are the parts of 'colligere' (to gather)

translation: Of these things, nothing was not only not accomplished, but not even planned.
